name: B.S. in Surveying Engineering Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan builds a coherent geomatics/surveying spine: SET 200 (Introduction to Geomatics) -> 207 evidence/procedures and 301 route surveying -> 303 photogrammetry, 304 adjustment computations, 307 boundaries, 360 digital surveying -> 401 geodesy, 405 reality capture, 407 boundary-line analysis, 420 GIS/LIS, 440 land development, 480 hydrographic, culminating in SET 490 Senior Project. Supporting math/stats (MATH 138/238/305), law/business (MGMT 290, ACCT, MIS 245) and CET 317/413 are well placed for a licensure-oriented surveying track.
dimensions.suggestion: Cluster the 'None' elective/GER rows into named requirement groups so the licensure-focused SET sequence is visually distinct from support courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy (deepest chain 6, e.g., SET 440 <- SET 307 <- SET 207) with a mix of prerequisites and co-requisites appropriate to field surveying. The 4 dangling references (GER 200/300, PHYS 102A, SET 200A) are placeholders/labs. One internal reference to check is SET 401 <- 'SET 302', a course not shown in the plan of study.
dimensions.suggestion: Verify and expose the SET 401 <- SET 302 (Adjustment Computations II) prerequisite and resolve the GER/lab placeholders so the chain is fully traceable.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data; only the plan and course descriptions are present. For a program aligned to professional-surveyor licensure this omission is a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes mapped to surveying-licensure competencies (boundary law, measurement science and adjustment, geodesy/GNSS, mapping and GIS).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120 = statedTotalCredits 120, delta 0.0), terms are loaded at 14-16 credits, and SET 407 is correctly weighted at 4 credits. The balance across measurement science, boundary/legal, mapping/GIS, and support courses is transparent.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credit-summary table separating the SET professional core from math-science and business/legal support so the distribution is visible at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and descriptions are complete and specific, but several unlabeled 'None' rows appear and a few prerequisite/co-requisite notes carry parsing noise (SET 207 'Co-rerequisites' typo; SET 301 co-requisite phrasing), leaving minor rough edges.
dimensions.suggestion: Label the blank elective/GER rows and clean the co-requisite typos so the plan reads cleanly.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is notably current for geomatics: reality capture and point clouds (SET 405), digital surveying with robotic total stations (SET 360), GIS/LIS (SET 420), photogrammetry (SET 303), hydrographic mapping (SET 480), geodesy and GNSS (SET 401, with GPS in SET 200). The main gaps are a dedicated UAS/drone-mapping course and deeper LiDAR and geospatial-programming content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or require a UAS/drone-surveying course (NJIT already offers ENGR 423 Drone Science) and deepen LiDAR within SET 405.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 33 sampled courses carry descriptions and the SET entries are specific and modern (SET 405 on collecting 3D point clouds and reality-capture technologies; SET 420 on vector-vs-raster GIS data models and cadastres; SET 304 on reducing and adjusting survey observations). Descriptions clearly convey scope and skills.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally explicit with grade/consent conditions and sensible depth (SET 407 <- SET 307; SET 440 <- 'SET 207 and CE 321'). Detractors are the SET 401 <- SET 302 reference not shown in the plan and a few co-requisite phrasing artifacts, plus the standard GER/lab placeholders.

missingTopics.rationale: SET 303 covers aerial photography but there is no dedicated unmanned-aircraft mapping course, now standard practice; ENGR 423 Drone Science could be adopted.
SET 405 introduces point clouds/reality capture; a dedicated terrestrial/mobile LiDAR course would deepen this fast-growing competency.

missingTopics.topic: Advanced GNSS positioning (RTK, network corrections, PPP)
missingTopics.rationale: SET 401 geodesy touches GNSS, but there is no course focused on real-time/precise satellite positioning workflows.
CS 106 and CET 317 provide only general computing; Python-for-GIS and spatial SQL are absent from the SET sequence.
SET 405 reality capture stops short of converting point clouds into BIM models, an increasingly required deliverable.

emergingTrends.rationale: SET 405 positions the program to lead on point-cloud-to-digital-twin workflows for the built environment.
Drone photogrammetry and autonomous data collection are becoming baseline surveying skills.

emergingTrends.trend: Cloud GIS and geospatial big data
emergingTrends.rationale: SET 420 could extend to cloud GIS and large-scale spatial analytics/geospatial AI.

emergingTrends.trend: Machine learning for point-cloud and feature extraction
emergingTrends.rationale: Automated classification of LiDAR/point-cloud data is a rising need connected to SET 405/433-style content.

emergingTrends.trend: Precise real-time positioning for autonomous systems
emergingTrends.rationale: RTK networks and PPP underpin autonomous vehicles and machine control, extending SET 401.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes mapped to surveying-licensure competencies.
Add/require a UAS/drone-surveying course and deepen LiDAR within SET 405.
Introduce geospatial programming (Python for GIS) and spatial databases.
Verify and expose the SET 401 <- SET 302 prerequisite and clean the co-requisite typos.
Modernize SET 420 toward cloud GIS and geospatial AI, and resolve GER/lab placeholders.
